column2: The way a worker uses posture, alignment, and movement to perform physical tasks with the least strain on the body.
column4: Good body mechanics turn a lift into a leg push instead of a back pull.

column2: The point in the body around which weight is evenly distributed; in a standing adult it sits just below the navel.
column4: Keeping your center of gravity over your feet is what prevents a loss of balance during a transfer.

column2: The area beneath the body bounded by the outside edges of the feet, which determines how stable a stance is.
column4: Widening your base of support by stepping your feet apart makes a lift feel more solid.

column2: A spine held in its natural slight curves, without rounding, twisting, or hyperextension.
column4: Bending at the hips and knees keeps your neutral spine while you lower yourself to the patient.

column2: The act of moving a patient from one surface to another, such as bed to chair.
column4: A stand-pivot transfer works only when the patient can bear weight on at least one leg.

column2: A transfer in which the patient stands, pivots on their feet, and sits on the new surface with staff support.
column4: Because the stand-pivot transfer depends on the patient's leg strength, it is not used for someone who cannot bear weight.

column2: An injury or disorder of the muscles, tendons, ligaments, joints, or spinal discs caused by workplace effort over time.
column4: A musculoskeletal disorder often develops from repeated small strains rather than one dramatic lift.

column2: Injury that builds up gradually from many small exposures, not from any single event.
column4: The back pain nurses develop mid-career is usually cumulative trauma from thousands of unassisted boosts.

column2: A program that replaces manual lifting with mechanical devices and trained techniques to reduce worker and patient injury.
column4: A hospital with a safe patient handling program provides equipment and trains staff to use it on every qualifying lift.

column2: A workplace rule that staff do not manually lift patients who cannot help themselves; mechanical lifts are used instead.
column4: Under a zero-lift policy, a 180-pound dependent patient is moved with a ceiling lift, not by two aides.

column2: A powered device (ceiling-mounted or floor-based) that lifts a patient using a sling under the body.
column4: A mechanical lift turns a two-person transfer into a one-person task that does not load the worker's spine.

column2: A slide sheet, board, or air-assisted mat that lowers the force needed to move a patient across a surface.
column4: Using a friction-reducing device to boost a patient up in bed cuts the effort by more than half.
